title: Pavlova
subtitle: Large meringue with a crisp shell and soft marshmallow centre
excerpt: A large baked meringue disc with a crisp outer shell and a soft, yielding marshmallow interior. Cream of tartar stabilises the egg whites; low heat and slow cooling keep the centre soft. Top with whipped cream and seasonal fruit before serving. Six to eight portions.
sourceNotes: Meringue-based desserts are documented in European domestic cooking from the seventeenth century. The pavlova, a large meringue disc with a soft interior topped with whipped cream and fruit, is named after the Russian ballerina Anna Pavlova and documented in Australian and New Zealand domestic cookbooks from the 1920s onward. The technique of using cream of tartar or an acid to stabilise egg white foam is documented in Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg, and Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, where the importance of a very clean bowl and the role of acid in producing a stable, firm foam are both noted.

content.text: Pavlova looks fragile and complicated and is neither. The meringue is straightforward if the bowl is spotlessly clean and fat-free, even a trace of egg yolk or butter will prevent the whites from foaming. The technique that distinguishes a pavlova from a crunchy meringue is how it is cooled: slowly, in the oven, so the inside stays soft. Cracks on the surface are normal. Pile them with cream and hide them.

items.name: Egg whites
items.prepNote: large, about 200 g, at room temperature

content.text: Preheat the oven to 120°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper and draw a 24 cm circle on it, then flip the paper over (so the pencil line doesn't touch the meringue). Ensure the metal bowl and beaters are completely clean and free of grease. Beat the {{egg-whites}} with the {{cream-of-tartar}} at medium speed until they reach stiff peaks, the foam should hold its shape when you lift the beaters. With the mixer running, add the {{caster-sugar}} one tablespoon at a time, waiting 10 seconds between additions. Once all the sugar is in, beat at high speed for 5 minutes until the meringue is thick, glossy, and feels smooth when you rub a little between your fingers. Beat in the {{vanilla-extract}}.

Spoon the meringue onto the prepared circle. Build it up at the edges so the centre is slightly lower, this creates a natural bowl for the cream. Smooth the sides with a palette knife or the back of a spoon. Bake for 90 to 95 minutes. The meringue should feel dry and crisp on the outside when tapped. Turn the oven off, open the door slightly, and leave the pavlova inside for 60 minutes. This is the
: the slow cool keeps the interior soft. Slide onto a rack and leave until completely cold.
Whip the {{double-cream}} with the {{icing-sugar}} to soft peaks. Pile it into the centre of the pavlova just before serving. Add seasonal fruit. Serve immediately.
